skill.name: swarm-advanced
skill.description: Advanced orchestration guidance for Claude Flow swarms across research, development, testing, and distributed workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and coordinate advanced Claude Flow swarm workflows for complex multi-agent tasks.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution and network patterns, but most are Markdown examples for Claude Flow MCP calls rather than executable code. The real risk is instructional: the skill tells users to install an external alpha package and register an MCP server, so publishing is acceptable with a warning. The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance hits were false positives caused by ordinary text, headings, and comments.

medium_findings.title: Instructional External Package Installation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run npm install globally and register a Claude Flow MCP server through npx. This is legitimate setup guidance, but it can execute third-party code if a user follows it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are a bash setup block with npm and npx commands. The risk is user-directed execution of an external package, not hidden code execution inside the skill.

medium_findings.title: Broad Orchestration Examples Include Automated Workflows
medium_findings.description: The workflow example shows reusable orchestration with commit and scheduled triggers. This can be useful, but users should review permissions and scope before enabling automated agents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example is clearly documentation, but automated triggers can cause repeated agent actions if copied into a live workflow. No malicious behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick findings, but the evidence is Markdown code fences and JavaScript examples. These are not executable files and do not run when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number inspection shows fenced Markdown blocks and sample MCP calls. There is no Ruby file, shell script, or dynamic command execution code in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with ordinary prose, comments, or identifiers such as description, design, development, or deploy. No cryptographic algorithm use was found in the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines do not contain MD5, SHA1, DES usage, or cryptographic operations. They contain skill metadata and workflow planning text.

low_findings.title: Reference Links to External Documentation
low_findings.description: The network findings are GitHub documentation URLs in the References section. They do not perform network requests from code, but users leave the marketplace if they open them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown links to GitHub documentation. No code sends data to those URLs or fetches resources automatically.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is Benign Guidance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hit points to best-practice text about avoiding overlapping responsibilities. No host inspection, environment probing, or system inventory behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain workflow advice. It does not invoke commands or request host, network, or account information.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Executed Setup Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes setup commands that install and run a community package. Users should inspect package provenance before executing these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and easy to verify, but they are setup instructions rather than hidden execution. Risk depends on whether the user chooses to run them.

content.value_statement: Complex projects need clear coordination across research, development, testing, and reporting work. This skill provides Claude Flow swarm patterns that help Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan multi-agent workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains mesh, hierarchical, star, and ring swarm topologies.
Provides research, development, testing, and optimization workflow patterns.
Shows how to spawn specialized Claude Flow agents for defined roles.
Demonstrates memory, monitoring, quality, and recovery patterns.
Includes examples for reports, full-stack builds, security audits, and performance work.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow setup before MCP examples can run.
Uses documentation examples instead of a packaged automation script.
Assumes users understand agent permissions and workflow boundaries.
Does not verify the safety of external packages or linked resources.

use_cases.title: Research Program Coordination
use_cases.description: Coordinate researchers, analysts, and writers to gather evidence, validate sources, and produce structured reports.

use_cases.title: Full-Stack Delivery Planning
use_cases.description: Split architecture, backend, frontend, database, testing, review, and deployment tasks across specialized agents.

use_cases.title: Quality and Security Review
use_cases.description: Organize testing, vulnerability analysis, performance checks, and reporting into a repeatable swarm workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Swarm Topology
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced skill to choose a topology for this project: [describe project]. Explain the agent roles and execution strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Research Swarm
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced skill to design a research swarm for [topic]. Include agent roles, phases, validation steps, and final outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Development Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced skill to create a multi-agent development plan for [feature]. Include architecture, implementation, tests, review, and documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Complex Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-advanced skill to audit this workflow for bottlenecks: [workflow]. Recommend topology changes, monitoring points, and recovery steps.

output_examples.input: Plan a research swarm for a market analysis project.
output_examples.output: A mesh topology with web research, source validation, data analysis, trend detection, and report writing agents. The plan separates collection, validation, synthesis, and delivery phases.

output_examples.input: Create a development swarm for a new API and dashboard.
output_examples.output: A hierarchical topology with an architect coordinator, backend and frontend developers, a database engineer, QA, reviewer, documenter, and DevOps support.
A star topology with parallel test execution, central monitoring, failure triage, performance metrics, and a recovery path for blocked test groups.

best_practices: Map each agent to a clear responsibility before starting the swarm.
Use parallel execution only for tasks without strong dependencies.
Review external setup commands and package sources before running them.
anti_patterns: Do not spawn many agents without a clear coordination model.
Do not automate commit or deployment triggers without permission review.
Do not store sensitive project data in shared memory without retention controls.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps plan advanced Claude Flow swarm workflows for research, development, testing, and optimization tasks.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide, but it includes setup commands that users may choose to run.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes instructions to install and run an external community MCP package. That requires user review.

faq.question: Can I use it with Codex and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What topology should I start with?
faq.answer: Use mesh for research, hierarchical for development, star for testing, and ring for staged pipelines.

faq.question: Does it replace project management?
faq.answer: No. It provides orchestration patterns, but teams still need clear goals, permissions, reviews, and ownership.
